summary: Gruber covers Judge Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers's devastating ruling that Apple willfully violated her 2021 court order requiring the App Store to allow developers to steer users to web purchases. The ruling reveals that Apple's finance team, led by CFO Luca Maestri and VP Alex Roman, convinced Tim Cook to impose a 27% commission on off-app purchases and erect new barriers — directly defying the injunction — while Phil Schiller advocated for genuine compliance. The judge found that Roman lied under oath and has referred the matter for potential criminal contempt proceedings. Gruber notes the ruling essentially validates Schiller's position all along: Apple should compete fairly with web purchases rather than obstruct them. He argues the business impact may be limited, but the reputational damage is severe, tempered only by Schiller's continued presence at Apple.
key_points: Apple imposed a 27% commission on off-app web purchases and erected scare screens to maintain its revenue stream, directly defying the court's anti-steering injunction
Phil Schiller advocated compliance and opposed the commission, but Tim Cook sided with CFO Luca Maestri and VP Alex Roman — the judge found Cook 'chose poorly'
Alex Roman lied under oath about when Apple decided on the 27% fee and whether they analyzed competitors' costs, and the judge referred the matter for criminal contempt investigation
The ruling stems from the Epic v. Apple case that Apple largely won — the injunction was a narrow remedy requiring only that apps be allowed to link users to web purchases
Gruber questions whether Maestri's departure as CFO was truly a planned succession or a consequence of this debacle
money_quotes: This is an injunction, not a negotiation. There are no do-overs once a party willfully disregards a court order.
Rule one when you're in court, any court, is 'Don't piss off the judge.' Apple has absolutely infuriated Gonzales Rogers through actions that all of us saw as outrageous.
You know a judge is pissed when she busts out the bold italics.
I mean Jesus H. Christ, that's scathing.

The only people in the world who seemed to think Apple could or should comply with the 2021 injunction by charging a commission on those web transactions were Apple's finance team members, led by Luca Maestri and Alex Roman, and Tim Cook.
As for Alex Roman, I think he's in some serious trouble. Like doing-time-in-the-clink trouble.
Are the results of this disastrous for Apple's reputation and credibility? It sure seems like it. But it would be worse — much worse — for Apple's reputation if Phil Schiller weren't still there.
For this Court, there is no second bite at the apple. It Is So Ordered.
key_insight: Apple's finance-driven decision to defy a court order with a 27% web commission — overruling Phil Schiller's counsel to compete fairly — has resulted in a devastating ruling that damages Apple's credibility far more than genuine compliance ever would have.
